title: Today's AI Brief: Nvidia chases $200B CPU market with AI agent PCs from Microsoft, Dell, and HP
summary: Nvidia unveils AI agent PCs, OpenAI models on AWS, and the impact on the AI landscape
imageAlt: Image from source article for Nvidia chases $200B CPU market with AI agent PCs from Microsoft, Dell, and HP
imageCaption: Nvidia chases $200B CPU market with AI agent PCs from Microsoft, Dell, and HP
imageSourceTitle: Nvidia chases $200B CPU market with AI agent PCs from Microsoft, Dell, and HP

body.text: I'm looking at some big moves in the AI space. Nvidia kicked off Computex in Taipei with a bang, unveiling a new PC CPU called the RTX Spark, dubbed a 'superchip.' This chip is designed to bring AI capabilities directly to PCs, and it's getting a lot of attention from major players like Microsoft, Dell, and HP. My read is that Nvidia is making a serious play for the $200 billion CPU market.

The RTX Spark is Nvidia's bid to make AI-powered PCs a reality. By partnering with major PC makers, Nvidia is positioning itself to be at the forefront of this shift. What's interesting here is that AI on PCs isn't just about fancy hardware – it's about enabling new use cases and experiences that weren't possible before. Think of it like having a super-smart assistant built right into your computer.

In other news, OpenAI's frontier models and Codex are now generally available on AWS. This is a big deal because it gives enterprises a new way to build with OpenAI's technology, using the AWS environments and workflows they're already familiar with. It's like adding a powerful new tool to the AWS toolbox – and it opens up all sorts of possibilities for businesses looking to tap into AI.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
